summary: Gruber corrects an assumption he made in his MacBook Neo review — that hardware camera indicator lights are inherently more secure than on-display indicators. Apple's Platform Security Guide reveals that the MacBook Neo's on-screen camera indicator runs inside a secure exclave on the A18 Pro chip, isolated from the kernel and macOS entirely. This means even a kernel-level exploit cannot enable the camera without the indicator appearing. Gruber uses expert context from developer Guilherme Rambo to explain the architecture, and points readers to a deeper resource on Apple's exclave evolution.
key_points: Gruber admits he was wrong in his initial MacBook Neo review, where he presumed dedicated hardware indicator lights were more secure than on-display ones.
Apple's on-screen camera indicator runs in a secure exclave — a completely isolated realtime OS with a minimal API surface, distinct from both the Secure Enclave and the main kernel.
Because it blits directly to screen hardware from a privileged isolated environment, even root or kernel-level exploits cannot suppress the indicator.
Secure exclaves are the culmination of a decade of Apple Silicon architecture work, only fully enabled with M4 and A18 generations.
The exclave-based approach also covers the microphone indicator, which hardware-based designs do not — a security bonus.
money_quotes: One might presume that the dedicated indicator lights are significantly more secure than the rendered-on-display indicators. I myself made this presumption in the initial version of my MacBook Neo review last week. This presumption is, I believe, wrong.

The architecture is designed to prevent any untrusted software — even with root or kernel privileges in macOS — from engaging the camera without also visibly lighting the on-screen camera indicator light.
Even a kernel-level exploit would not be able to turn on the camera without the light appearing on screen.
It runs in a privileged environment separate from the kernel and blits the light directly onto the screen hardware.
All of that applies to the mic indicator as well, which is a bonus compared to the camera-only hardware indicator.
Exclaves run on a completely isolated realtime operating system that communicates with the kernel and userspace using a very limited API surface.
That's right, his text message had a footnote.
key_insight: Apple's on-screen camera indicator on the MacBook Neo is as secure as a hardware light — not despite being software, but because it runs in a kernel-isolated secure exclave that cannot be overridden by even root-level exploits.
